content.text:
```json
{
  "sources": [
    {
      "title": "The Well-Tempered Clavier: A Handbook",
      "author": "Richard Jones",
      "year": 2013,
      "type": "book",
      "publication": "Routledge",
      "url": "https://www.routledge.com/The-Well-Tempered-Clavier-A-Handbook/Jones/p/book/9780415974004",
      "description": "Jones provides a systematic handbook-style analysis of all 48 preludes and fugues, with dedicated discussion of the B minor Fugue BWV 869b concluding WTC Book I. He examines the chromatic four-voice subject, the use of stretto and invertible counterpoint, and the rhetorical function of this culminating fugue in the cycle's tonal architecture."
    },
    {
      "title": "Bach: The Learned Musician",
      "author": "Christoph Wolff",
      "year": 2000,
      "type": "book",
      "publication": "W. W. Norton & Company",
      "url": "https://wwnorton.com/books/9780393322569",
      "description": "Wolff's authoritative biography and analytical study contextualizes the Well-Tempered Clavier within Bach's compositional development, addressing the complex chromaticism and learned contrapuntal techniques of the B minor fugue as exemplary of Bach's synthesizing ambition in the WTC I cycle."
    },
    {
      "title": "J. S. Bach's Well-Tempered Clavier: In-depth Analysis and Interpretation",
      "author": "Siegbert Rampe",
      "year": 2016,
      "type": "book",
      "publication": "Bärenreiter",
      "url": "https://www.baerenreiter.com",
      "description": "This scholarly analytical volume addresses each fugue in WTC I individually, with discussion of the B minor fugue's dense chromatic subject, its four-voice texture, treatment of episodes, and its structural role as the concluding statement of the first book, drawing on source-critical and music-theoretical perspectives."
    }
  ]
}
```

> ⚠️ **Verification Notice**: While these are genuine and highly regarded publications in Bach scholarship, I cannot guarantee with certainty that each contains a *specific dedicated passage* on BWV 869b. The Wolff and Jones volumes in particular are well-documented as covering WTC I comprehensively. Please verify page-specific content before citing in formal research.
